prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
I think you're probably wrong about what it means to know something. 

When we say we know something, we often mean we have a high degree of confidence in the truth of the statement. But knowledge in the philosophical sense involves more than just justified true belief. To truly know something, you also need to be aware of the basis for your belief and that basis needs to be sufficiently robust to count as knowledge. 

Many beliefs we think of as knowledge are actually just opinions or assumptions that end up being false. Consider how our theories in science keep evolving as we learn more. Theories once seen as unassailable, like Newton's ideas about absolute space and time, were overturned. So even in cutting-edge science, our supposed knowledge is provisional.

On a more mundane level, think about how many times in your life you were convinced you knew something only to later discover you were mistaken. Whether it's events in your own life, facts about the world, or beliefs about other people - many things we think we know turn out to be off the mark. The more we examine the basis for our beliefs, the more we may start to doubt their supposed certainty. 

So while you may feel very confident in many things you believe, I think digging deeper into the philosophy of knowledge shows that few of our beliefs actually qualify as the kind of unassailable knowledge we often imagine we have. Most are mere opinions that may be right or may be wrong. That's just a thought. Let me know what you think!
